perturbation.original_text: (e) The term “ Affiliate ” means any Person controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, any other Person; “control” shall mean the ownership of, with right to vote, 50% or more of the outstanding voting securities, equity, membership interests, or equivalent, of such Person.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) The term “ Affiliate ” means any Person controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, any other Person; “control” shall mean the ownership of, with right to vote, 25% or more of the outstanding voting securities, equity, membership interests, or equivalent, of such Person.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an internal contradiction in the definition of 'Affiliate'. Initially, 'control' required 50% ownership, but now it requires only 25%. This contradiction is relevant throughout the document where 'Affiliate' status affects obligations and permitted actions, especially regarding information disclosure and liability. Because the definition of Affiliate has changed from 50% ownership to 25% ownership, this in-text contradiction creates ambiguity throughout the legal document on the exact meaning of Affiliate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: §1. Certain Definitions . (a) The term “ Information ” means (i) all financial, technical and other non-public or proprietary information which is furnished or disclosed orally, in writing, electronically or in other form or media by Disclosing Party and/or its Representatives to Recipient and/or its Representatives in connection with the Purpose and that is described or identified (at the time of disclosure) as being non-public, confidential or proprietary, or the non-public or proprietary nature of which is apparent from the context of the disclosure or the contents or nature of the information disclosed; (ii) all memoranda, notes, reports, files, copies, extracts, inventions, discoveries, improvements or any other thing prepared or derived from the information described in §1(a)(i), above; (iii) all CEII (as such term is defined below and only if CEII is exchanged under this Non-Disclosure Agreement); (iv) all Personal Information (as defined in the ISA Exhibit and only if Personal Information is exchanged under this Non-Disclosure Agreement); and (v) all Customer Information (as such term in is defined below and only if Customer Information is exchanged under this Non-Disclosure Agreement). (b) The term “ Recipient ” means a Party to whom the other Party or its Representatives discloses Information. (c) The term “ Disclosing Party ” means the Party disclosing Information in its possession, or on whose behalf Information is disclosed, to a Recipient. - 2 - (d) The term “ Representative(s) ” means the Affiliates of a Party and the officers, directors, members, managers, employees, contractors, legal advisors, financial advisors and representatives of such Party and its Affiliates. (e) The term “ Affiliate ” means any Person controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, any other Person; “control” shall mean the ownership of, with right to vote, 25% or more of the outstanding voting securities, equity, membership interests, or equivalent, of such Person. (f) The term “ Customer Information ” includes, but is not limited to, one or more National Grid customers’ names, addresses, account numbers, billing information, load information, and usage information. (g) The term “Person” includes any natural person, individual, firm, corporation, company, partnership (general or limited), limited liability company, business trust, joint venture, consortium, government or political subdivision, or any agency, instrumentality, or authority of any government or political subdivision, or other entity or association.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Affiliate' from requiring 50% ownership to only 25%. This creates a direct in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which relies on this definition of 'Affiliate' to define 'Representative(s)'. The change directly conflicts with the contradicted text by changing a crucial component of the definition used to establish who constitutes a 'Representative' which impacts obligations and rights related to information disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall receive all Information in strict confidence, shall exercise reasonable care to maintain the confidentiality and secrecy of the Information, and, except to the extent expressly permitted by this Non-Disclosure Agreement, shall not divulge Information to any third party without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall receive all Information in strict confidence, but is only obligated to exercise reasonable effort to maintain the confidentiality and secrecy of the Information. The Recipient is not liable for disclosure due to cyber attacks. Except to the extent expressly permitted by this Non-Disclosure Agreement, Recipient shall not divulge Information to any third party without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Originally, the recipient 'shall exercise reasonable care'. Now, the recipient is only obligated to 'exercise reasonable effort'. The added clause regarding cyber attacks provides an additional loophole. This directly contradicts the liability sections of the ISA Exhibit, creating ambiguity about the level of security required and who bears the responsibility for breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.16 Contractor agrees that, to the fullest extent permitted by law, it shall be and remain strictly liable for the security of all Personal Information when in Contractor’s possession and when being transmitted from Contractor or received by Contractor. Without limiting any other obligations under any agreement entered into between the Parties, Contractor agrees that it shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless National Grid and its Affiliates and their officers, directors, employees, agents, servants, successors and assigns, from and against any and all claims, losses, demands, liabilities, costs and other expenses (including but not limited to, reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs, administrative penalties and fines, costs expended to notify individuals and/or to prevent or remedy possible identity theft, financial harm or any other claims of harm related to a breach) incurred as a result of, or arising directly out of or in connection with any acts or omissions of Contractor or any party under its control, including, but not limited to, negligent or intentional acts or omissions, resulting from a Security Breach or encryption failure in the transmission of such Personal Information, except to the extent such act or omission is caused by the sole negligence of National Grid. This provision shall survive termination of this Addendum, the Non-Disclosure Agreement and any other agreement between the Parties relevant to the Purpose.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the recipient's obligation to maintain confidentiality from 'shall exercise reasonable care' to 'exercise reasonable effort' and introduces an exception for cyber attacks. This contradicts section 2.16 of the ISA Exhibit, which states that the contractor 'shall be and remain strictly liable for the security of all Personal Information' and must indemnify National Grid for any losses resulting from a security breach. The original text maintains strict liability, while the changed text introduces loopholes, creating a direct conflict and ambiguity about the level of security required and who bears responsibility, meeting the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall return and deliver, or cause to be returned and delivered, to Disclosing Party, or destroy or cause to be destroyed (with certification of destruction delivered to Disclosing Party), all tangible Information, including copies and abstracts thereof, within thirty (30) days of a written request by Disclosing Party (a “ Request ”).
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall return and deliver, or cause to be returned and delivered, to Disclosing Party, or destroy or cause to be destroyed (without certification of destruction delivered to Disclosing Party), all tangible Information, including copies and abstracts thereof, within ninety (90) days of a written request by Disclosing Party (a “ Request ”).
perturbation.explanation: The original term mandates return or destruction of all tangible Information within thirty (30) days. The modified text extends this period to ninety (90) days and removes the certification requirement. This is a contradiction. If National Grid has a data breach that is being investigated, and that needs to be quickly investigated by the disclosing party, the changed version gives 90 days and no proof that the deletion was done. The ISA exhibit section 3.1 calls for written certification of destruction, in the change, such certification is removed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.  0 DATA SCRUBBING VERIFICATION 3.1 Upon termination of all agreements between the Parties relevant to and in connection with the Purpose, Contractor shall return to National Grid all Personal Information or destroy such Personal Information beyond recovery and certify such destruction in writing to National Grid. Without limiting the foregoing, upon termination of all agreements between the Parties relevant to and in connection with the Purpose, Contractor shall use the best possible means to scrub, or otherwise destroy beyond recovery all electronic Personal Information in its possession, certifying such destruction in writing to National Grid’s procurement agent, and providing National Grid with a written explanation of the method used for data disposal/destruction, along with a written certification that such method meets or exceeds the National Grid’s data handling standards and industry best practices for the disposal/destruction of sensitive data. If such return or destruction is not feasible, Contractor shall provide to National Grid notification of the conditions that make return or destruction infeasible. Upon National Grid’s written agreement that return or destruction of Personal Information is infeasible, Contractor shall extend the protections of this Addendum to such Personal Information and limit further uses and disclosures of such Personal Information to those purposes that make the return or destruction infeasible, for so long as Contractor maintains such Personal Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for returning or destroying tangible information from 30 to 90 days and removes the requirement for certification of destruction. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which mandates that upon termination of agreements, the contractor must certify in writing to National Grid the destruction of Personal Information. The removal of the certification requirement creates a strong contradiction, as the contradicted section explicitly requires written certification.

perturbation.original_text: Except with respect to any Information that is Customer Information, CEII or Personal Information, Recipient’s obligations and duties under this Non-Disclosure Agreement shall have a term of [ ] [()] years from the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), but in no event will the confidentiality obligations herein terminate less than one (1) year from the date of the last disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Except with respect to any Information that is Customer Information, CEII or Personal Information, Recipient’s obligations and duties under this Non-Disclosure Agreement shall have a term of [ ] [()] years from the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), but the confidentiality obligations herein may terminate in less than one (1) year from the date of the last disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states confidentiality obligations will not terminate less than one year from the last disclosure, while the modified clause suggests they *may* terminate sooner. This directly creates a contradiction and therefore ambiguity on the actual period of confidentiality. It also affects all other sections which may rely on one year from the last disclosure. All sections about obligations have changed because the amount of time has changed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: §9. Term and Termination . Except with respect to any Information that is Customer Information, CEII or Personal Information, Recipient’s obligations and duties under this Non-Disclosure Agreement shall have a term of [ ] [()] years from the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), but the confidentiality obligations herein may terminate in less than one (1) year from the date of the last disclosure. In the case of any Information that is Customer Information, CEII or Personal Information, Recipient’s obligations and duties under this Non-Disclosure Agreement shall survive for (i) the Term, or (ii) so long as such Customer Information, CEII or Personal Information, as applicable, is required to be kept confidential under applicable law, whichever period is longer (the “ Special Information Term ”). Either Party may terminate this Non-Disclosure Agreement by written notice to the other Party. Notwithstanding any such termination, all rights and obligations hereunder shall survive (i) for the Special Information Term for all Customer Information, CEII or Personal Information disclosed prior to such termination, and (ii) for the Term for all other Information disclosed prior to such termination.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the confidentiality obligations would not terminate less than one year from the last disclosure. The changed text states that the obligations 'may' terminate in less than one year. The contradicted text, which is identical to the changed text, now includes this contradictory statement. The change creates a direct conflict because it introduces uncertainty about the minimum duration of confidentiality obligations, thus fulfilling the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Non-Disclosure Agreement may be amended or modified only by an instrument in writing signed by authorized representatives of all Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Non-Disclosure Agreement may be amended or modified only by an instrument in writing signed by authorized representatives of one Party.
perturbation.explanation: Originally, all parties were required to amend the document. The modified text changes this to only one party being required. This introduces ambiguity in the document about if all parties or one party is required to amend or modify the agreement. The legal document requires all parties to sign and agree on something to amend the document but this changes that and has a contradiction on who needs to agree in order to amend it. It causes ambiguity on section §15's 'Entire agreement', since with one signature, a party could say the agreement is changed with this clause and there is no entire agreement, leading to ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: §15. Entire Agreement . This Non-Disclosure Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof, and any and all previous representations or - 6 - agreements with respect to such subject matter, either oral or written, are hereby annulled and superseded.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that all parties must sign to amend the agreement. The changed text reduces this requirement to only one party. This directly contradicts §15, which states that the agreement constitutes the entire agreement 'between the Parties.' If only one party can amend the agreement, it's unclear whether the 'entire agreement' still reflects the consensus of all parties. Therefore, the change creates a strong in-text contradiction as it introduces ambiguity about the validity and completeness of the 'Entire Agreement' defined in §15, since a single party's signature can alter the entire agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor shall provide National Grid with a list of all subcontractors and vendors that will have access to National Grid’s Confidential and/or Personal Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor may provide National Grid with a list of some subcontractors and vendors that will have access to National Grid’s Confidential and/or Personal Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause, the Contractor is required to give ALL names of subcontractors and vendors. The modified text changes this to a few names. This introduces ambiguity about National Grid understanding all the possible parties that can access the confidentional data which could violate the law. This directly contradicts the ISA exhibit on article 2.11 since National Grid wouldn't know, with certainty, which subcontractors have National Grid information and it wouldn't be able to enforce that they agree to certain restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.  11 Contractor agrees to ensure that any subcontractor or vendor to which it provides National Grid’s Information, including Personal Information received from National Grid, or to which it provides National Grid’s Information and/or Personal Information created or received by Contractor on behalf of National Grid, agrees to the same restrictions and conditions set forth herein through a written contractual agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies 'Contractor shall provide' to 'Contractor may provide' and 'all subcontractors and vendors' to 'some subcontractors and vendors'. This introduces ambiguity as National Grid cannot be certain which subcontractors have access to confidential information. This directly contradicts clause 2.11 which requires the Contractor to ensure that ANY subcontractor or vendor agrees to the same restrictions. If the contractor only provides some names, it is impossible for National Grid to enforce the restrictions on all relevant parties, therefore a strong contradiction exists.
